4 min read
Just now

--

One of the best beginner projects for learning web development is creating a calculator. Although it looks simple, it helps you understand how HTML structures a webpage, how CSS improves its appearance, and how JavaScript makes it interactive.
In this article, I’ll explain how I built a basic calculator from scratch using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
Project Structure
The project contains three files:

• index.html — Creates the structure of the calculator.

• style.css — Styles the calculator and makes it visually appealing.

• script.js — Handles all the calculator’s functionality.
Keeping each file separate makes the project easier to understand, maintain, and update.
Creating the Calculator with HTML
The first step was creating the calculator’s structure using HTML.
Inside the <body>, I created a main <div> that acts as the calculator's container. This keeps every part of the calculator organized inside one parent element.
Inside this container, I created a <form>. Rather than using the form to submit data, I used it to organize the calculator's display and buttons.
The form contains:

• A display input where numbers and operators appear.

• Five <div> elements that organize the calculator buttons into rows.

• Multiple <input> buttons representing:

• Numbers (0–9)

• Mathematical operators (+, −, ×, ÷)

• Decimal point

• Clear button

• Delete button

• Equal (=) button
Grouping the buttons into rows makes the HTML cleaner and much easier to style with CSS.
Each button performs a specific action when clicked, which is later handled using JavaScript.
Styling the Calculator with CSS
After building the structure, I used CSS to transform the plain HTML into a modern calculator.
First, I centered the calculator on the page using Flexbox. This ensures the calculator stays in the middle of the screen regardless of the device size.
These small styling choices make the calculator look more polished and professional.
Next, I styled the display input by giving it:

• A larger font size

• Right-aligned text so numbers appear naturally

• Adequate padding

• A clean border
For the calculator buttons, I applied consistent:

• Width and height

• Font size

• Margin and spacing

• Border radius

• Cursor pointer
I also added hover effects so users receive visual feedback whenever they move their mouse over a button. This improves the overall user experience by making the interface feel more interactive.
CSS doesn’t make the calculator work, but it makes it pleasant and easy to use.
Making the Calculator Functional with JavaScript
The real functionality of the calculator comes from JavaScript.
Without JavaScript, the calculator would simply display buttons without performing any calculations.
Using the onclick Event
I used the onclick event on each button.
Whenever a user clicks a number or an operator, JavaScript immediately responds to that action.
For example, when the user clicks 7, JavaScript adds the value 7 to the calculator display.
When the user clicks +, the plus operator is also added to the display.
This creates the mathematical expression one button at a time.
Instead of manually typing into the display, every button updates it automatically.
The code works by appending the clicked button’s value to the display using:
display.value += this.value;Here:

• display.value represents the text currently shown on the calculator screen.

• this.value refers to the value of the button that was clicked.

• The += operator appends the new value to the existing expression instead of replacing it.
For example:

• Click 5 → Display shows 5

• Click + → Display shows 5+

• Click 9 → Display shows 5+9
JavaScript builds the complete expression as the user presses different buttons.

Clearing the Display
The Clear (AC) button removes everything currently displayed.
When clicked, JavaScript simply resets the display to an empty string.
display.value = "";This allows the user to start a new calculation whenever needed.

Deleting the Last Character
The Delete button is slightly different from the Clear button.
Instead of removing everything, it removes only the last character entered.
To achieve this, I used JavaScript string methods.
Since everything displayed on the calculator is treated as a string, JavaScript can remove the last character using:
display.value = display.value.toString().slice(0, -1);Here’s how it works:

• toString() ensures the display is treated as text.

• slice(0, -1) creates a new string that contains every character except the last one.
For example:
Before:
345+After pressing Delete:
345This makes correcting typing mistakes much easier.

Performing Calculations with eval()
Once the user finishes entering an expression and presses the Equal (=) button, JavaScript calculates the result.
I used JavaScript’s built-in eval() function for this.
display.value = eval(display.value);The eval() function reads the entire mathematical expression stored in the display and evaluates it as JavaScript code.
For example:
25+15becomes
40Another example:
12*8becomes
96Because eval() understands standard mathematical operators, it can quickly calculate the final result without requiring complex logic.
Although eval() is convenient for beginner projects like this calculator, developers generally avoid using it in larger applications because it can execute arbitrary JavaScript code if given untrusted input. In production applications, it's safer to use dedicated expression parsers or implement the calculation logic directly.
This project also helped me become more comfortable with concepts such as events, DOM manipulation, strings, and JavaScript functions.
Although it’s a beginner-friendly project, creating a calculator is an excellent way to practice the core skills every front-end developer needs. It demonstrates how structure, design, and functionality combine to produce an interactive web application.
Thank you for reading.
